title: Advanced Array Methods
tagline: Transform data like a pro with map, reduce, and filter.

accomplishments: Transform an array of numbers into an array of their doubles
Convert an array of products into an array of formatted prices
Build a temperature converter that transforms entire datasets

do.title: Double Every Number
steps: Click each button and watch the transformations!
do.code:
🔄 The Map Method

Original Array:
[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
Transformed Array:
Click a button to transform
do.result: .map() took every number in the array and transformed it according to the function you provided!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing the squared numbers result [1, 4, 9, 16, 25].

concepts.name: What is .map()?
concepts.definition: Transforms every item in an array and returns a NEW array with the transformed values. The original array stays unchanged. array.map(function(item) { return transformed; })
concepts.analogy: An assembly line. Each item goes in, gets transformed, comes out changed. The original raw materials are still there — you get a new collection of finished products.

concepts.name: .map() returns a NEW array
concepts.definition: Unlike .push() or .pop(), .map() doesn't modify the original array. It creates a brand new array. You must store the result: let doubled = numbers.map(...).
concepts.analogy: Taking a photo of a painting — the original painting is unchanged, you just have a new copy to work with.

concepts.name: Arrow function syntax
concepts.definition: => replaces the word function for cleaner code. numbers.map(num => num * 2) is the same as numbers.map(function(num) { return num * 2; }). Single-line arrow functions auto-return.
concepts.analogy: Shorthand — like writing "btw" instead of "by the way." Same meaning, less typing.

concepts.name: Extracting properties from objects
concepts.definition: Common pattern: users.map(user => user.name) extracts just the names from an array of user objects. Great for pulling specific data out of complex arrays.
concepts.analogy: Going through a stack of business cards and copying just the phone numbers onto a new list.

concepts.name: Chaining array methods
concepts.definition: Because .map() returns a new array, you can chain it with other methods: input.split('\n').map(parseFloat).filter(n => !isNaN(n)). Each step transforms the result of the previous.
concepts.analogy: A factory assembly line with multiple stations — cut, paint, polish, package. Each step passes its output to the next.

understand.keyTakeaway: Use .map() when you need to transform every item in an array. It creates a new array, leaves the original alone, and makes code cleaner than for loops.

build.goal: Build a temperature converter that transforms arrays of temperatures between Celsius and Fahrenheit
build.hint: Formula for C to F: (C * 9/5) + 32. Formula for F to C: (F - 32) * 5/9. Use .toFixed(1) to round to 1 decimal place.

challenge.title: Build a Price Formatter
challenge.mission: Create a product price formatter that applies different transformations (add tax, apply discount, convert currency) using .map().
requirements: Array of at least 5 products (name, price)
"Add Tax (10%)" button multiplies prices by 1.10
"Apply Discount (20%)" button multiplies prices by 0.80
"Convert to EUR" button multiplies prices by 0.92
Display original and transformed prices side-by-side
Use .map() for all transformations
Format prices with $ or € symbols
challenge.starterCode: 💰 Price Formatter
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add a "Reset" button that shows original prices, and a combo button that applies discount THEN adds tax!

capabilities: Use .map() to transform every item in an array
Understand that .map() creates a new array
Write arrow functions for cleaner code
Extract properties from arrays of objects

bridge.preview: In the next lesson, you'll learn .forEach() for side effects, .reduce() for aggregating data, and when to use each array method.

accomplishments: Understand why some variables are accessible and others aren't
Create private variables that only your functions can access
Build a counter that remembers its state
Prevent variable naming conflicts in large projects

do.title: See Scope in Action
steps: Click the buttons and watch the console
Notice which variables are accessible where!
do.code:
🔍 Scope Demonstration

Global Scope

Function Scope

Block Scope

Closure Example

💡 Open your browser console (F12) to see the results!
do.result: You saw how different scopes work! Global = everywhere, Local = inside function, Block = inside { }
do.proofRequired: Screenshot of the browser console showing the different scope results.

concepts.name: What is scope?
concepts.definition: Scope determines which parts of your code can see and use which variables. Variables declared in one place may or may not be accessible from another place.
concepts.analogy: Your code is a house. Global variables are like the WiFi password — everyone in every room can use it. Local variables are like items in a bedroom — only accessible in that room.

concepts.name: Global scope
concepts.definition: Variables declared outside all functions. Accessible EVERYWHERE in your code. Too many globals cause naming conflicts and bugs — use sparingly!
concepts.analogy: A billboard in the middle of town — everyone can see it, but if everyone writes on it, it gets messy fast.

concepts.name: Function scope (local)
concepts.definition: Variables declared inside a function. Only accessible INSIDE that function. Prevents naming conflicts — different functions can use the same variable name safely.
concepts.analogy: Notes in your personal notebook — only you can read them, and they don't interfere with anyone else's notes.

concepts.name: Block scope (let/const)
concepts.definition: Variables declared with let or const inside { } blocks (if, for, while). Only accessible inside that block. var does NOT have block scope — always use let/const.
concepts.analogy: A temporary whiteboard in a meeting room — it only exists while the meeting is happening.

concepts.name: The scope chain
concepts.definition: JavaScript looks for variables starting in the current scope, then moves OUTWARD to parent scopes. Inner can access outer, but outer cannot access inner.
concepts.analogy: Asking for help: ask your sibling first, then your parent, then your grandparent. But grandparents can't ask grandchildren!

concepts.name: What is a closure?
concepts.definition: When an inner function remembers and can access variables from its outer function, EVEN AFTER the outer function has finished running. The variables are "trapped" in the closure.
concepts.analogy: Giving someone a personalized stamp. You create the stamp with a custom message, then hand it over. Even though you're gone, the stamp still has that message embedded in it.

concepts.name: Closures create private data
concepts.definition: You can use closures to create variables that can ONLY be accessed through specific functions — they're private! This is how you build bank accounts, counters, and secure state in JavaScript.
concepts.analogy: A safe deposit box — the money inside can only be accessed through the bank's specific procedures. You can't just reach in and grab it.

understand.keyTakeaway: Scope controls variable access. Closures let inner functions remember outer variables. Together, they enable private data and powerful patterns.

build.goal: Build a counter factory that produces independent counters with private state
build.hint: The magic is in the closure! Each counter's count variable is trapped in the closure. The returned methods can access it, but nothing else can.

challenge.title: Build a Bank Account System with Private Balance
challenge.mission: Create a bank account factory with private balances. Users can deposit, withdraw, and check balance, but CANNOT directly access the balance variable.
requirements: Factory function createBankAccount(accountName, initialBalance)
Private balance variable (only accessible through methods)
Methods: deposit(amount), withdraw(amount), getBalance(), getAccountName()
Withdraw should fail if balance is insufficient
All amounts must be positive numbers
Track transaction history as a private array
getHistory() method returns transactions
Display multiple accounts on the page
Each account shows name, balance, and transaction history
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add a transfer() method that moves money from one account to another. Hint: pass another account object as a parameter.

capabilities: Understand global, function, and block scope
Use closures to create private variables
Build factory functions that return objects with methods
Create multiple independent instances with private state

bridge.preview: In the next lesson, you'll learn about ARROW FUNCTIONS and CALLBACKS — modern JavaScript syntax that makes your code cleaner and more powerful, especially when working with arrays.

accomplishments: Write concise arrow functions instead of long function syntax
Pass functions as arguments to other functions
Chain array methods to process data elegantly

do.title: Transform Data with Array Methods
steps: Click each button to see different transformations
Notice how much cleaner this is than loops!
do.code:
🚀 Modern Array Methods

📊 Original Data:

[
  { name: "Laptop", price: 999, category: "electronics" },
  { name: "Phone", price: 699, category: "electronics" },
  { name: "Desk", price: 299, category: "furniture" },
  { name: "Chair", price: 199, category: "furniture" },
  { name: "Monitor", price: 399, category: "electronics" }
]

1. .map() - Transform each item

2. .filter() - Select items that match

3. .reduce() - Combine into single value

4. Method Chaining
do.result: You transformed arrays with modern methods instead of loops. Much cleaner and more readable!

concepts.name: Arrow Functions Syntax
concepts.definition: A shorter way to write functions. const add = (a, b) => a + b replaces function add(a, b) { return a + b; }. Single parameter needs no parentheses: num => num * 2.
concepts.analogy: Shorthand like "btw" instead of "by the way" — same meaning, way less typing.

concepts.name: Callbacks
concepts.definition: A function passed as an argument to another function. Array methods like .map(), .filter(), and .reduce() all accept callback functions that determine what they do.
concepts.analogy: Telling someone "when you get there, do this" — you hand them instructions to follow later.

concepts.name: .map() - transform each item
concepts.definition: Creates a NEW array by calling a function on every element. array.map(item => transformed). Always returns an array of the same length as the original.
concepts.analogy: An assembly line — every item comes in, gets transformed, comes out changed.

concepts.name: .filter() - select items
concepts.definition: Creates a NEW array with only items that pass a test (return true). array.filter(item => condition). Can return a shorter or empty array.
concepts.analogy: A bouncer at a club — only lets in people who meet the criteria, sends the rest away.

concepts.name: .reduce() - combine to one value
concepts.definition: Reduces an array to a SINGLE value. array.reduce((accumulator, item) => newAccumulator, initialValue). Great for sums, averages, counting, flattening.
concepts.analogy: Squeezing a sponge — takes in a whole array and wrings it out into one result.

concepts.name: Method chaining
concepts.definition: Because .map() and .filter() return arrays, you can chain them: products.filter(p => p.category === "electronics").map(p => p.price).reduce((sum, p) => sum + p, 0). Each line is one transformation step.
concepts.analogy: An assembly line with multiple stations — each station passes its output to the next.

understand.keyTakeaway: Arrow functions are concise. Array methods (.map, .filter, .reduce) replace most loops. Chain them together for powerful data transformations.

build.goal: Build a product catalog with category and price filters using method chaining
build.hint: Use .map() to create HTML for each product, then .join('') to combine into one string for innerHTML.

challenge.title: Build a Student Grade Analyzer
challenge.mission: Create a grade analyzer that processes student data using ONLY arrow functions and array methods (no for loops!).
requirements: Array of student objects with: name, scores (array of test scores), attendance (%)
Calculate average score for each student using .reduce()
Use .map() to add grade property (A/B/C/D/F based on average)
Use .filter() to find students with average < 60 (need help)
Sort and slice to get top 3 students
Calculate class average using .reduce()
Find highest and lowest scores using Math.max/min with spread
All calculations must use arrow functions and array methods (NO for loops!)
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add a search feature that filters students by name using .filter() and arrow functions!

bridge.preview: In the final lesson of this module, you'll learn how to ORGANIZE large codebases — breaking code into modules, following best practices, and building maintainable applications.

accomplishments: Break a messy 500-line file into organized modules
Follow the DRY principle (Don't Repeat Yourself)
Create helper functions that make code readable
Build a complete app with professional code organization
Use design patterns that real developers use

do.title: Refactor Messy Code Into Organized Modules
steps: Copy the code below (notice how it's organized!)
Click around and see a fully functional app
Read the code — notice how EVERY function has ONE clear job
do.code:
✅ Professional Task Manager

Built with organized, maintainable code
do.result: This is how professionals organize code — separated by responsibility, easy to read, easy to maintain!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot of the task manager working with at least 3 tasks added.

concepts.name: DRY: Don't Repeat Yourself
concepts.definition: If you're writing the same code twice, make it a function instead. Fix bugs in one place. Add features in one place.
concepts.analogy: A recipe card — write it once, reuse it a thousand times instead of memorizing it fresh each time.

concepts.name: Single Responsibility Principle
concepts.definition: Each function should do ONE thing and do it well. If you find yourself describing a function with "and" or "also," split it up.
concepts.analogy: A specialized tool — a screwdriver doesn't also hammer nails. Each function is a single-purpose tool.

concepts.name: Separation of Concerns
concepts.definition: Group related code together. Separate unrelated code. State management, UI rendering, and event handling each belong in their own section.
concepts.analogy: A restaurant kitchen: prep station, grill, plating. Each station has one job, but they work together.

concepts.name: Helper functions
concepts.definition: Small utility functions that make your code more readable. isValidPrice(price) is clearer than checking null/undefined/NaN/>0 inline.
concepts.analogy: A label on a jar — you know what's inside without opening it. Helper functions label intent.

concepts.name: Pure functions
concepts.definition: Functions that always return the same output for the same input, with no side effects. Easy to test, debug, and reason about.
concepts.analogy: A math equation — 2 + 2 always equals 4, no matter what else is going on.

concepts.name: Function naming conventions
concepts.definition: get/set for data access, is/has/can for booleans, calculate/compute for math, handle for events, render/display/show for UI, validate/check for validation.
concepts.analogy: Street signs — you know what to expect from the name before you get there.

concepts.name: Code comments: WHY not WHAT
concepts.definition: Don't comment obvious things (// increment count). Do comment tricky logic, warnings, and function documentation. Good code is self-documenting.
concepts.analogy: A recipe doesn't say "crack egg" — it says "room temperature eggs work best" (the WHY).

concepts.name: The module pattern
concepts.definition: Organize related functions and data into a single object. const TaskManager = { tasks: [], add(), remove(), getAll() }. All related code in one namespace.
concepts.analogy: A toolbox — all the screwdrivers in one drawer, all the wrenches in another. Related tools together.

understand.keyTakeaway: Professional code is organized, readable, and maintainable. Each function does ONE thing. Code is grouped by purpose. Comments explain WHY, not WHAT.

build.goal: Understand how the 6-section structure makes code professional
build.hint: Each section has ONE purpose. Each function within a section has ONE job. Read your code out loud — if a function needs "and" to describe it, split it up.

challenge.title: Build a Complete Expense Tracker App (CAPSTONE)
challenge.mission: Build a full expense tracking application using ALL the organization principles. This is your capstone project for Module 4!
requirements: Organize code into 6 clear sections: State, Utilities, Data Operations, Statistics, UI Rendering, Event Handlers
Add expenses with: description, amount, category, date
Categories: Food, Transport, Entertainment, Bills, Other
Display all expenses in a list
Filter by category
Calculate and display: total expenses, this month's expenses, average expense
Delete expenses
Save to localStorage
Use helper functions: formatMoney(), isValidExpense(), calculateTotal()
Each function must have ONE clear responsibility
Add JSDoc comments to key functions
Make it look professional
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add an "Edit Expense" feature that lets users modify existing expenses. Requires edit mode UI, state updates, and re-rendering.

capabilities: Organize code into clear sections by responsibility
Write functions that do ONE thing well
Apply the DRY principle to eliminate repetition
Use helper functions to make code readable
Separate concerns: data, UI, and events
Use the module pattern for encapsulation

bridge.preview: 🎉 Module 4 complete! You've built buttons, text changers, theme switchers, score trackers, product catalogs, grade analyzers, task managers, and a full expense tracker. You now write JavaScript like a professional developer! The next modules would cover APIs, async JavaScript, and building with frameworks like React.
